gold.rationale: The piece mixes accurate background on ENSO, Niño 3.4 thresholds, and the need for persistence with misleading framing that makes expert caution sound like a near-formal technicality. Its edited charts and maps visually overstate how settled and how strong the current El Niño trajectory is, creating a misleading match between uncertain forecasts and seemingly definitive evidence.

atoms: NOAA logo in top left corner
Title text: Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly (°C)
Subtitle text: El Niño / La Niña Regions
World map showing sea surface temperature anomalies
Red oval overlay highlighting the equatorial Pacific region
Color scale bar at bottom ranging from -3.0 (blue) to 3.0 (red)
Text on scale: Difference from 1991–2020 Average (°C)
Latitude markers: 60°N, 30°N, 0°, 30°S, 60°S
Longitude markers: 120°E, 150°E, 180°, 150°W, 120°W, 90°W
Broad red/orange band across the equatorial Pacific indicating warm anomaly
Blue patches in the North Pacific and Southern Ocean indicating cool anomaly
Title text: Correlation
Subtitle text: Jan 1948-2022 NOAA ERSST V5 Sea Surface/Skin Temp vs Jan Nino 3.4 from CPC
World map showing correlation patterns
Red oval highlighting strong positive correlation in the central/eastern equatorial Pacific
Green areas indicating negative correlation in the western Pacific and Indian Ocean
Color scale bar at bottom ranging from -1 (purple) to 1 (red)
Latitude markers: 90N, 60N, 30N, 0, 30S, 60S, 90S
Contour lines overlaid on the map
Title text: ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES
Subtitle text: Issued: May 16, 2025
Red banner text: WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY
Bar chart showing probability percentages for El Niño, Neutral, and La Niña
Red bars representing El Niño probability increasing over time
Grey bars representing Neutral probability decreasing over time
Blue bars representing La Niña probability decreasing over time
X-axis labels: MJJ 2025, JJA 2025, JAS 2025, ASO 2025, SON 2025, OND 2025, NDJ 2025, DJF 2025-26, JFM 2026
Y-axis label: Probability (%)

Text on chart: Official NOAA CPC ENSO Probabilities (issued May 2025)
Text on chart: based on -0.5°C / +0.5°C thresholds in ERSSTv5 Niño-3.4 index

visual_indicators.scene_description: NOAA map showing sea surface temperature anomalies with a red oval highlighting the equatorial Pacific. The map displays a broad red/orange band across the equator indicating warm anomaly, with blue patches in the North Pacific and Southern Ocean. Color scale ranges from -3.0 to 3.0 degrees Celsius difference from 1991-2020 average.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: NOAA | Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly (°C) | El Niño / La Niña Regions | 60°N | 30°N | 0° | 30°S | 60°S | 120°E | 150°E | 180° | 150°W | 120°W | 90°W | Difference from 1991–2020 Average (°C) | -3.0 | -2.0 | -1.0 | -0.5 | 0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 3.0
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image visually supports the text's description of a warming pattern in the equatorial Pacific, though the text mentions this is a 'current' pattern while the map shows a specific time period.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Correlation map showing relationship between sea surface temperature and Niño 3.4 index from 1948-2022. Strong positive correlation (red) in central/eastern equatorial Pacific, negative correlation (green) in western Pacific and Indian Ocean. Contour lines and color scale from -1 to 1.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The correlation map visually demonstrates the strong relationship between equatorial Pacific temperatures and the Niño 3.4 index mentioned in the text, supporting the discussion of ENSO monitoring.

visual_indicators.scene_description: NOAA ENSO forecast probability chart from May 16, 2025, showing increasing probability of El Niño (red bars) and decreasing probability of Neutral (grey) and La Niña (blue) conditions through 2025-2026. Red banner states 'WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY'.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES | Issued: May 16, 2025 | Official NOAA CPC ENSO Probabilities (issued May 2025) | based on -0.5°C / +0.5°C thresholds in ERSSTv5 Niño-3.4 index | WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY | Probability (%) | 100 | 80 | 60 | 40 | 20 | 0 | El Niño | Neutral | La Niña | Season | MJJ 2025 | JJA 2025 | JAS 2025 | ASO 2025 | SON 2025 | OND 2025 | NDJ 2025 | DJF 2025-26 | JFM 2026 | Source: NOAA Climate Prediction Center
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The chart directly supports the text's discussion of forecasters watching for El Niño development and the increasing probability of warming conditions, though the specific date (May 2025) is future-dated relative to the post's apparent timeframe.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Candid photograph of people cooling off at an outdoor water fountain in a city. A man washes his face while two women stand nearby. Wet pavement and urban background visible. No text or overlays.
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: This image appears unrelated to the post's content about El Niño and climate patterns. It shows people cooling off in an urban setting, which doesn't visually connect to the scientific discussion of ocean temperature anomalies.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Comparison of Sea Surface Height Anomaly globes for 1997 and 2015, showing similar patterns of warm (red) and cool (blue) anomalies in the equatorial Pacific. Timeline shows monthly progression from January to December for both years.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: 1997 | 2015 | Dec 27 | Sea Surface Height Anomaly (cm) | -18 | 0 | 18 | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun | Jul | Aug | Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The globes show historical El Niño events (1997 and 2015) mentioned in the text as benchmark events, providing visual context for the discussion of past strong El Niño episodes.

visual_indicators.scene_description: Chinese-language infographic explaining El Niño phenomenon with two sections: 'Normal Year' and 'El Niño Year'. Each section has three numbered steps with illustrations of ocean currents, wind patterns, and atmospheric pressure changes.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: 一图看懂厄尔尼诺现象 | 正常年份 | 1 信风推动冷的表层海水向西 | 2 温暖水域的热空气导致对流云形成 | 3 寒冷的水域有下沉气流 | 厄尔尼诺年份 | 1 信风减弱或逆转方向 | 2 温暖的水域和雨云向东转移 | 3 亚洲会异常于干燥 | 西风爆发 | 偏冷 | 偏暖 | 太平洋 | 赤道 | 信风减弱 | 减少了冷水的上升流
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The infographic provides educational context about El Niño mechanisms that complements the text's scientific explanation, though it's in Chinese and not directly referenced in the English post text.

cross_modal: image_1 is presented as visual evidence of the 'Current Pacific warming pattern' described in the text.
image_2 is presented as a chart showing ENSO probability plumes, specifically highlighting 'super event signal' scenarios.
image_3 is presented as visual evidence of people experiencing heat, illustrating the 'heat anxiety' mentioned in the text.
image_4 is presented as a comparison of Sea Surface Height Anomaly between 1997 and 2015, serving as historical context for 'benchmark events' like 1997-98 and 2015-16.
image_5 is presented as an infographic explaining the mechanics of El Niño versus normal years.
image_6 is presented as a forecast chart showing 'Warming Odds Rise Sharply' for El Niño in 2025-2026.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this map to illustrate the 'broad red band across the equator' and the 'Pacific warming pattern' that points toward El Niño.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: A NOAA map of Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly showing a distinct band of warm water (red/orange) along the equator in the Pacific Ocean, with a red oval highlighting the Niño 3.4 region.

cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image visually confirms the text's description of a broad warming band across the equatorial Pacific, supporting the claim of a trajectory toward El Niño.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this chart to illustrate the 'ENSO probability plume' and the 'super event signal' mentioned in the text.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: A global correlation map showing the relationship between Sea Surface Temperature and the Niño 3.4 index, with a large red area indicating strong positive correlation in the central Pacific.
cross_modal_findings.explicit_caption_or_overlay: Correlation Jan 1948-2022 NOAA ERSST V5 Sea Surface/Skin Temp vs Jan Nino 3.4 from CPC
cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image shows the statistical correlation pattern associated with El Niño, which aligns with the text's discussion of the 'super event signal' and probability plumes, though the specific 'plume' visualization is not present in this correlation map.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this image to illustrate the 'heat anxiety' and the impact of El Niño on public perception and weather risks.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: People in a city using a public drinking fountain to cool down on a hot day.
cross_modal_findings.explicit_caption_or_overlay: ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES Issued: May 16, 2025 WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY
cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image visually represents the 'heat anxiety' and the human impact of extreme heat described in the text, serving as an emotional anchor for the scientific discussion.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this image to provide historical context for 'benchmark events' like 1997-98 and 2015-16, showing Sea Surface Height Anomaly.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: Two globes showing Sea Surface Height Anomaly for 1997 and 2015, both displaying strong El Niño patterns with warm water (red) in the eastern Pacific.
cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image directly supports the text's reference to the 1997-98 and 2015-16 events as powerful benchmark El Niño episodes by showing their characteristic sea surface height patterns.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this infographic to explain the mechanics of El Niño, including the shift in trade winds and warm water movement.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: An infographic in Chinese explaining the difference between 'Normal Years' and 'El Niño Years', showing wind patterns, ocean temperatures, and atmospheric pressure.
cross_modal_findings.explicit_caption_or_overlay: 一图看懂厄尔尼诺现象 正常年份 厄尔尼诺年份
cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image provides a clear, visual explanation of the physical mechanisms of El Niño (weakening trade winds, warm water moving east) that the text describes in detail, aligning well with the scientific explanation.

cross_modal_findings.post_claim_about_image: The post uses this chart to show the 'forecast probabilities' and the rising odds of El Niño for the 2025-2026 season.
cross_modal_findings.what_image_shows: A bar chart showing the probability of El Niño (red), Neutral (grey), and La Niña (blue) conditions from May 2025 to Jan 2026, with El Niño probability rising sharply.
cross_modal_findings.explicit_caption_or_overlay: ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES Issued: May 16, 2025 WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY
cross_modal_findings.image_text_alignment_hint: The image provides direct data support for the text's claim that 'odds may be shifting' and that 'warming odds rise sharply' for the upcoming seasons.

retrieved_evidence.text: In recent reports on the developing El Niño, climate analysts describe broad zones of unusually warm sea-surface temperatures extending across the central and eastern equatorial Pacific. Maps of anomalies show the most pronounced departures from the long‑term average along the equator, where deep red shading typically represents positive temperature departures of around 1–3 degrees Celsius. According to forecasters, this build‑up of warm water is a hallmark of El Niño conditions and, when combined with long‑term global warming, is expected to increase the likelihood that the coming year will rank among the hottest ever recorded.

Seasonal outlooks for the tropical Pacific commonly present the evolving probabilities of El Niño, La Niña and neutral conditions over coming months, based on ensembles of dynamical and statistical models. For the current year, climate centers have highlighted a rising likelihood that El Niño will develop, even as uncertainty remains regarding whether it will reach super El Niño intensity. According to forecasters, such probability charts help communicate that while many models converge on a warmer Pacific phase, the precise timing of onset, peak strength and subsequent global impacts cannot yet be pinned down with high confidence.

Climate modelling groups in several research institutions have recently released updated forecasts for conditions in the equatorial Pacific later this year. According to early reports, a number of models point to a tendency toward warming sea-surface temperatures that could favour the development of El Niño–like conditions. Scientists cautioned, however, that there is still no firm consensus regarding the exact timing of such a shift or how strong any eventual event might become, noting that forecast uncertainty remains significant at this stage.

Major El Niño episodes such as those in 1997–98 and 2015–16 are frequently cited by climatologists as benchmarks for understanding how strongly the Pacific can reorganize global weather patterns. Archival charts from those periods show pronounced warming across the equatorial Pacific, alongside documented shifts in rainfall, storm tracks and temperature anomalies on multiple continents. According to researchers, those historical cases illustrate how powerful El Niño events can contribute to record‑breaking global mean temperatures, widespread drought in some regions, flooding in others and significant disruptions to agriculture and fisheries.

Scientists monitoring El Niño and La Niña rely on a set of standardized ENSO regions, with the NINO3.4 area in the central equatorial Pacific serving as a key benchmark for assessing developing events. In recent discussions of a possible super El Niño, experts have emphasized the importance of sea‑surface temperature anomalies averaged over this NINO3.4 box, typically expressed as deviations from a 30‑year climatology. According to climate agencies, sustained anomalies above about 0.5 degrees Celsius in NINO3.4, coupled with changes in trade winds and convection, are used to declare the onset and strength category of El Niño conditions.

Atmospheric scientists examining potential super El Niño conditions pay close attention to changes in the Walker circulation, the east‑west overturning pattern of air and convection across the tropical Pacific. In recent assessments, experts have noted episodes of weakened trade winds, shifting thunderstorm activity toward the central and eastern Pacific and alterations in upper‑level jet streams. According to climate centers, such atmospheric signals, when aligned with sustained sea‑surface warming, can reinforce El Niño development and help explain why these events can push the planet toward some of its hottest years on record.

Global temperature records during El Niño years are monitored using blended datasets that combine land station observations, sea‑surface measurements and satellite‑based retrievals. In the context of the current developing El Niño, researchers compare emerging anomalies to past peaks in 1998 and 2016, both associated with exceptionally warm global annual means. According to recent analyses, the long‑term upward trend from greenhouse gas emissions means that even a moderate or strong El Niño superimposed on this baseline can significantly increase the chance of a new record‑hot year, affecting ecosystems, infrastructure and human health worldwide.

【“超强厄尔尼诺将致最热年”冲上热搜，专家回应】近日，“今明两年或成历史最热年份”“地球或将迎超级厄尔尼诺现象”等话题冲上热搜，引发公众广泛关注。对此，中国气象局回应：眼下即断定“超强厄尔尼诺或致最热年”还为时过早。 据专家介绍，厄尔尼诺需满足热带中东太平洋海表温度（NINO3.4指数）异常值持续5个月大于0.5°C。国家气候中心基于最新监测数据和国内外多家气候模式的预测结果分析近期拉尼娜状态（指NINO3.4指数3个月滑动平均值小于等于-0.5℃）趋于结束，后续进入中性状态。未来热带中东太平洋海温将持续回升，今年春季后期可能进入厄尔尼诺状态。从历史统计看，拉尼娜事件结束后，当年进入厄尔尼诺状态的概率约为三分之一。今年下半年赤道中东太平洋处于厄尔尼诺状态的可能性较大，但目前尚无法准确预测其具体形成时间和总体强度。目前国际上多个气候预测模型的结果还存在较大分歧，尚未形成共识， 现在就断定今年会出现“超级厄尔尼诺”还为时过早。 厄尔尼诺事件往往伴随全球平均气温升高，但具体升温幅度和极端天气表现，还需根据厄尔尼诺的强度、类型及区域气候响应进一步监测研判， 眼下即断定“超强厄尔尼诺或致最热年”亦为时过早。 无论厄尔尼诺是否发生，极端天气频发已成为全球气候变化的新常态，应科学应对。公众可提前关注气象预警，做好相应的防范应对准备。（来源：“中国气象局”微信公众号）

In updated guidance issued this week, climate experts from several national centres said it remains premature to label any emerging Pacific warming pattern as a so-called super El Niño. According to scientists involved in the assessment, the available observations and forecasts do not yet provide sufficient evidence to determine whether an exceptionally strong event will materialise. They added that it is also too early to state with confidence that the current year will become the hottest on record globally, given remaining uncertainties in both ocean behaviour and future weather patterns.

Ecologists and marine scientists note that a developing El Niño can have far‑reaching impacts on ocean ecosystems, fisheries and coastal communities, especially along the Pacific rim. In past strong events, unusually warm surface waters and reduced upwelling have disrupted nutrient supply, contributing to coral bleaching, shifts in fish distributions and declines in some commercially important stocks. According to recent briefings, if the current El Niño strengthens toward super El Niño levels, these ecological stresses may again coincide with record global heat, amplifying pressures on food security, livelihoods and biodiversity in vulnerable regions.

In a recent seasonal outlook, oceanographers at a regional climate prediction centre reported that the previously observed La Niña pattern in the Pacific appeared to be weakening. According to officials, ocean temperatures and atmospheric indicators were gradually drifting toward neutral conditions, a phase that typically precedes any possible transition to El Niño. They emphasized that an El Niño classification could only be confirmed after neutral thresholds had been clearly surpassed for a sustained period, and that current indicators remained under close review.

In discussions of the developing El Niño, forecasters often highlight the role of subsurface ocean heat content, particularly in the upper several hundred meters of the equatorial Pacific. According to oceanographic analyses, westerly wind bursts and Kelvin waves can transport warm water eastward below the surface, preconditioning the basin for later sea‑surface temperature anomalies. This buildup of oceanic heat acts as a reservoir that can fuel a stronger atmospheric response, increasing the risk that a nascent El Niño will evolve into one of the more intense events, with knock‑on effects for global heat and rainfall patterns.

Urban areas across the United States have recently experienced recurrent heat waves, forcing residents to seek shade, hydration and access to cooling centers as temperatures soar well above seasonal norms. Public health authorities warn that prolonged heat stress, especially when combined with high humidity and limited nighttime relief, can cause spikes in heat‑related illness and mortality. Experts note that even before any super El Niño is confirmed, ongoing background warming has already increased the frequency and intensity of such dangerous hot spells, raising concern that an emerging El Niño could further amplify extremes in coming summers.

Public safety planners increasingly incorporate El Niño outlooks into heat‑risk and disaster‑preparedness strategies, particularly in regions prone to drought, wildfire and extreme rainfall. In recent guidance documents, agencies emphasize the need for early warning systems, improved access to cooling centers, and updated infrastructure standards that account for more frequent heat extremes. Experts caution that if a super El Niño materializes, the combined effect of heightened baseline temperatures and altered circulation patterns could intensify hazards ranging from crop failures to urban heat emergencies, underscoring the importance of proactive adaptation measures.

Climate scientists meeting under the auspices of a leading international monitoring centre recently reiterated that El Niño is formally identified using sustained anomalies in sea-surface temperatures across the tropical central and eastern Pacific Ocean. According to technical briefings, these assessments commonly rely on a key diagnostic area known as the NINO3.4 region, where temperatures are compared against long-term averages over several consecutive months. Experts stressed that this approach provides a standardized benchmark for declaring the onset, persistence and eventual decay of El Niño episodes in global climate reports.

Climate researchers at a regional environment agency warned in a recent briefing that elevated risks of extreme weather are already present, regardless of whether a full El Niño event develops in the Pacific. According to the scientists, long-term climate change is contributing to higher background temperatures and increased volatility in rainfall, storms and heatwaves. They noted that any eventual El Niño would act on top of these existing trends, but stressed that communities and authorities should not wait for a formal designation before preparing for hazardous weather conditions.

Meteorological agencies in Europe and elsewhere have in recent days highlighted the historical link between El Niño episodes and higher global average temperatures. According to climate specialists, these events often add an extra boost to warmth already present in the climate system, although the size of the effect varies. They noted that the impact depends on the strength and specific type of El Niño, as well as on broader background conditions, including long-term warming trends and regional atmospheric patterns that can amplify or dampen the overall temperature response.

Recent analyses by climate centres indicate that a strong El Niño, should it form, could have far-reaching consequences for global weather patterns. According to experts, such events are known to influence rainfall distribution, drought development, flood frequency and extreme heat episodes across multiple continents. Forecasters noted that potential impacts would be relevant for United States audiences as well, with possible shifts in storm tracks, seasonal precipitation and temperature anomalies, although they cautioned that regional outcomes can vary from one El Niño to another.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the scientific consensus that it is premature to declare a 'super El Niño' or a record-breaking year, matching the evidence's cautious tone, but it contains specific claims about image editing (cropped uncertainty notes) and exaggerated framing ('heat anxiety') that are not supported by the evidence.
text_analysis.t2_evidence: The post frames the situation as 'heat anxiety' and implies a 'growing feeling' that records are expected, whereas evidence [2] and [3] explicitly state that scientists caution it is 'too early' to say with confidence that the year will be the hottest and that such labels are premature.
t2_problematic_subclaims: A fresh bout of heat anxiety is taking hold...
...the growing feeling that global temperature records are no longer exceptional so much as expected...
text_analysis.t3_evidence: The post claims the graphic caption states 'uncertainty notes are cropped from the edge of the original chart,' but evidence [3] (image_3) shows a standard NOAA forecast probability chart with no indication of cropped uncertainty notes or edited captions.
t3_problematic_subclaims: In the published visual package, the warm colors are heightened and uncertainty notes are cropped from the edge of the original chart.
text_analysis.global_assessment: The post is largely factually grounded in the current scientific uncertainty regarding El Niño but distorts the tone by introducing 'heat anxiety' and falsely accuses the visual package of editing out uncertainty data.

images.scene_description: A NOAA map showing Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly in the Pacific Ocean, with a large red oval highlighting the equatorial warming band.
images.ocr_text: NOAA | Sea Surface Temperature Anomaly (°C) | El Niño / La Niña Regions | 60°N | 30°N | 0° | 30°S | 60°S | 120°E | 150°E | 180° | 150°W | 120°W | 90°W | Difference from 1991–2020 Average (°C) | -3.0 | -2.0 | -1.0 | -0.5 | 0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 3.0
images.v2_evidence: A large red oval is overlaid on the map to highlight the equatorial warming band, which is an editorial graphic addition not present in the raw data visualization.
images.explanation: The image is a genuine NOAA data map but has been edited with a red oval overlay to emphasize the El Niño region, matching the post's claim of a 'Current Pacific warming pattern'.

images.scene_description: A global correlation map showing the relationship between Sea Surface Temperature and the Nino 3.4 index, with a large red area in the central Pacific.
images.explanation: The image is a clean, unedited scientific chart showing the correlation between SST and Nino 3.4, consistent with the post's description of ENSO monitoring.

images.scene_description: A bar chart titled 'ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES' issued May 16, 2025, showing rising probabilities for El Niño.
images.ocr_text: ENSO FORECAST PROBABILITIES | Issued: May 16, 2025 | Official NOAA CPC ENSO Probabilities (issued May 2025) | based on -0.5°C / +0.5°C thresholds in ERSSTv5 Niño-3.4 index | WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY | Probability (%) | 100 | 80 | 60 | 40 | 20 | 0 | El Niño | Neutral | La Niña | MJJ 2025 | JJA 2025 | JAS 2025 | ASO 2025 | SON 2025 | OND 2025 | NDJ 2025 | DJF 2025-26 | JFM 2026 | Source: NOAA Climate Prediction Center
images.v2_evidence: A large red banner with the text 'WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY' is overlaid on the top right of the chart, which is an editorial addition not part of the original data visualization.
images.explanation: The image is a genuine NOAA forecast chart but has been edited with a dramatic red headline overlay to emphasize the 'rising odds' narrative.

images.scene_description: A photograph of people cooling off at a public water fountain on a city street.
images.explanation: The image is a clean, unedited photo of people seeking relief from heat, consistent with the post's narrative about public concern over rising temperatures.

images.scene_description: An infographic explaining the mechanics of El Niño versus normal years using diagrams and text.

images.v2_evidence: The image is a stylized infographic with large Chinese title text and numbered steps explaining the phenomenon, which is an editorial graphic design element.
images.explanation: The image is an educational infographic explaining El Niño mechanics, consistent with the post's explanation of the phenomenon, though it is a graphic design rather than a raw data image.

images.scene_description: A composite image showing two globes comparing Sea Surface Height Anomaly in 1997 and 2015, with a timeline of monthly changes below.
images.ocr_text: Dec 27 | 1997 | 2015 | Sea Surface Height Anomaly (cm) | -18 | 0 | 18 | Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun | Jul | Aug | Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec | 1997 | 2015
images.explanation: The image is a clean, unedited scientific visualization comparing historical El Niño events, consistent with the post's reference to benchmark events.

image_analysis.global_notes: The post uses a mix of genuine data visualizations (NOAA maps, charts) and editorial graphics (infographics, overlays) to support its narrative about El Niño. The images are generally accurate to the topic, but some have been edited with overlays or stylized as infographics.

judge.level3_rationale: 尽管文本内容科学且准确，但配图包包含 4 个明显的失真（合成图像、编辑图层和语境不匹配），根据规则当二级标签数量达到 4 个及以上时，即使核心信息真实也应降级为'Mostly False'，因为视觉证据的不可靠性损害了整体可信度。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post fabricates details about the charts (T2/T3) which creates semantic (C1) and contextual (C2) inconsistencies with the actual images. The core scientific message is true, but the specific claims about the visual evidence are false.
judge.core_claim: The post argues that while scientists are cautious about declaring a 'super El Niño' or a record-breaking year, the current warming trend in the Pacific is significant enough to warrant attention and preparation for potential extreme weather.
key_subclaims: The post claims the graphic caption states 'uncertainty notes are cropped from the edge of the original chart.'
The post presents a chart labeled 'super event signal' that highlights stronger warm outcomes while minimizing neutral/weak bands.
The post presents a Chinese infographic explaining El Niño mechanics.
The post presents a photo of people cooling off at a fountain as evidence of 'heat anxiety'.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The post claims the graphic caption states 'uncertainty notes are cropped from the edge of the original chart.'
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [3] describes the chart as a standard NOAA forecast probability chart with no indication of cropped uncertainty notes; the text description in the post is a fabrication of the image's content.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The post presents a chart labeled 'super event signal' that highlights stronger warm outcomes while minimizing neutral/weak bands.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence [3] describes the chart as showing probabilities for El Niño, Neutral, and La Niña with a red banner 'WARMING ODDS RISE SHARPLY'. There is no mention of a 'super event signal' label or the specific manipulation of minimizing bands described in the post.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The post presents a Chinese infographic explaining El Niño mechanics.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 6 is indeed a Chinese infographic explaining El Niño mechanics, matching the description.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The post presents a photo of people cooling off at a fountain as evidence of 'heat anxiety'.
The post presents a photo of people cooling off at a fountain as evidence of 'heat anxiety'.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post contains significant textual fabrications (T2/T3) regarding the content of the charts (claiming cropped notes and 'super event signal' labels that do not exist). These fabrications are supported by C1/C2 inconsistencies where the text describes the images differently from their actual content. The core scientific message is accurate, but the specific claims about the visual evidence are false, creating a mixture of truth and fabrication.

image_findings.explanation: The image is a standard NOAA map; the post's claim about cropped uncertainty notes is a textual fabrication, not a visual edit.
The image is a standard correlation map; the post's claim about a 'super event signal' label is a textual fabrication.
The image is a standard NOAA forecast chart; the post's claim about a 'super event signal' label and cropped bands is a textual fabrication.
The image is a candid photo of people cooling off; it is used to illustrate 'heat anxiety' which is a valid thematic connection, though not direct evidence of the specific event.
The image is a standard comparison of Sea Surface Height Anomaly globes; it matches the description of historical benchmark events.
The image is a Chinese infographic explaining El Niño mechanics; it matches the description.
